findings.title: Secret detected: Detected a Generic API Key, potentially exposing access to various services and sensitive operations.
findings.description: Betterleaks detected a Detected a Generic API Key, potentially exposing access to various services and sensitive operations. in battle_test_candidates/go_target/main.go:31. Rule: generic-api-key. Entropy: 4.92
findings.tool_output: Rule: generic-api-key | Tags: [] | Entropy: 4.92 | Verified: false

findings.title: Potential G704: SSRF via taint analysis
findings.description:
SSRF via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the 'url' parameter in the HTTP request
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends GET request to /fetch?url=http://internal-service:8080/admin 2. Handler extracts 'url' parameter (line 155) 3. http.Get(targetURL) is called (line 157) 4. Server makes request to internal service 5. Response is returned to attacker
attack_scenario.impact: attacker can probe internal network services (e.g., cloud metadata endpoints, internal APIs) and read their responses, potentially gaining access to sensitive data or credentials
attack_scenario.explanation: The code directly uses user-supplied URL without any allowlist or validation, enabling SSRF. The comment confirms vulnerability.

findings.title: Potential G701: SQL injection via taint analysis
findings.description:
SQL injection via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
findings.code_snippet:
71 | 70: 	query := "SELECT id, username, role FROM users WHERE username='" + username + "' AND password='" + password + "'"
71: 	row := db.QueryRow(query)
72:

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the 'username' and 'password' parameters in the HTTP request
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends POST request to /login with username=' OR '1'='1' -- and password=anything 2. Handler extracts username and password (lines 68-69) 3. String concatenation builds query (line 70) 4. db.QueryRow executes malicious query (line 71) 5. Attacker bypasses authentication
attack_scenario.impact: attacker can bypass authentication and log in as any user, or extract arbitrary data from the users table via UNION-based SQL injection
attack_scenario.explanation: Direct string concatenation of user input into SQL query without parameterization. Classic SQL injection.

findings.title: Potential G703: Path traversal via taint analysis
findings.description:
Path traversal via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the 'filename' parameter in the HTTP request
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends GET request to /read?filename=../../etc/passwd 2. Handler extracts filename (line 134) 3. filepath becomes './files/../../etc/passwd' (line 135) 4. os.ReadFile reads /etc/passwd (line 136) 5. Attacker receives file contents
attack_scenario.impact: attacker can read arbitrary files on the server filesystem, including configuration files, source code, or system files like /etc/shadow
attack_scenario.explanation: User-controlled filename is concatenated into a file path without sanitization, allowing directory traversal.

findings.title: Potential G702: Command injection via taint analysis
findings.description:
Command injection via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
[Also reported by another step: Subprocess launched with variable
Gosec confidence: 90%]

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the 'cmd' parameter in the HTTP request
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends GET request to /exec?cmd=ls;cat%20/etc/passwd 2. Handler extracts cmd (line 112) 3. exec.Command('bash', '-c', cmd) is called (line 114) 4. Shell executes arbitrary commands 5. Output is returned to attacker
attack_scenario.impact: attacker can execute arbitrary system commands on the server, leading to full server compromise, data exfiltration, or lateral movement
attack_scenario.explanation: User input is passed directly to bash -c, enabling command injection. The comment confirms vulnerability.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the 'input' parameter in the HTTP request
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends GET request to /hash?input=<script>alert(1)</script> 2. Handler extracts input (line 204) 3. fmt.Fprintf writes JSON with unsanitized input (line 210) 4. If response is rendered in a browser with Content-Type application/json, XSS may be limited; but if the response is embedded in HTML (e.g., via fetch), script executes
attack_scenario.impact: attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser, potentially stealing cookies, session tokens, or performing actions on behalf of the user
attack_scenario.explanation: User input is reflected in JSON response without encoding. While Content-Type is JSON, some browsers may still execute script if the response is loaded in a script context.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — Content-Type application/octet-stream forces download, preventing script execution in browser. XSS requires HTML rendering.]

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the 'filename' parameter
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends request to /read?filename=malicious.html 2. Handler reads file (line 136) 3. w.Write(data) writes raw bytes (line 143) 4. Content-Type is application/octet-stream, so browser will download file, not render HTML
attack_scenario.impact: no XSS because Content-Type forces download; file is not rendered as HTML
attack_scenario.explanation: Content-Type application/octet-stream forces download, preventing script execution in browser. XSS requires HTML rendering.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — Content-Type text/plain ensures browser treats response as plain text, not HTML, so no script execution.]

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the 'cmd' parameter
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends request to /exec?cmd=echo%20<script> 2. Handler executes command (line 114) 3. w.Write(out) writes output (line 121) 4. Content-Type is text/plain, so browser renders as plain text, not HTML
attack_scenario.impact: no XSS because Content-Type text/plain prevents script execution
attack_scenario.explanation: Content-Type text/plain ensures browser treats response as plain text, not HTML, so no script execution.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the 'html' parameter in the HTTP request
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends GET request to /render?html=<script>alert(1)</script> 2. Handler extracts html (line 98) 3. w.Write([]byte(html)) writes raw HTML (line 100) 4. Content-Type is not set, defaults to text/html 5. Browser renders and executes script
attack_scenario.impact: attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in victim's browser, leading to session hijacking, phishing, or defacement
attack_scenario.explanation: User input is written directly as HTML response without any encoding or sanitization. Content-Type defaults to text/html, enabling XSS.

findings.title: Potential G401: Use of weak cryptographic primitive
findings.description:
Use of weak cryptographic primitive
Gosec confidence: 90%
findings.code_snippet:
206 | 205: 	// VULNERABLE: MD5 is cryptographically broken
206: 	hash := md5.Sum([]byte(input))
207: 	hexHash := fmt.Sprintf("%x", hash)

attack_scenario.actor: attacker who can obtain MD5 hashes (e.g., via SQL injection or other means)
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker obtains MD5 hash of a password from database 2. Uses rainbow tables or collision attacks to find preimage 3. MD5 is cryptographically broken, collisions can be generated in seconds
attack_scenario.impact: attacker can reverse weak passwords or create hash collisions, potentially compromising user accounts or forging authentication tokens
attack_scenario.explanation: MD5 is considered weak due to collision attacks and fast computation. Use SHA-256 or stronger.

findings.title: Potential G114: Use of net/http serve function that has no support for setting timeouts
findings.description:
Use of net/http serve function that has no support for setting timeouts
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: remote attacker who can establish many connections to the server
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker opens many slow HTTP connections (slowloris) 2. Server has no read/write timeouts 3. Connections remain open, exhausting goroutines/file descriptors 4. Server becomes unresponsive to legitimate requests
attack_scenario.impact: denial of service: server can be taken offline by exhausting resources, impacting availability
attack_scenario.explanation: http.ListenAndServe with nil handler uses default server with no timeouts, making it vulnerable to slowloris and resource exhaustion attacks.

attack_scenario.actor: unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the 'username' and 'password' parameters
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends POST to /login with malicious input 2. String concatenation builds SQL query (line 70) 3. db.QueryRow executes (line 71) 4. SQL injection occurs
attack_scenario.impact: attacker can bypass authentication or extract arbitrary data from database
attack_scenario.explanation: Same as IRON-GOSEC-002; SQL string concatenation.

findings.title: Potential G501: Blocklisted import crypto/md5: weak cryptographic primitive
findings.description:
Blocklisted import crypto/md5: weak cryptographic primitive
Gosec confidence: 90%
findings.code_snippet:
11 | 10: import (
11: 	"crypto/md5"
12: 	"database/sql"

attack_scenario.actor: attacker who can exploit weak crypto (e.g., via hash collision or preimage)
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker obtains MD5 hash from application 2. Uses collision attack to generate two inputs with same hash 3. Forges authentication tokens or bypasses integrity checks
attack_scenario.impact: attacker can forge data or bypass integrity checks due to weak cryptographic primitive
attack_scenario.explanation: Importing crypto/md5 is a weak cryptographic primitive; use SHA-256 or stronger.

findings.title: Potential Generic API key in config
findings.description: Regex pattern detected a potential Generic API key in config at main.go:31. This may be a hardcoded secret that automated scanners missed.
findings.code_snippet: 31 | const apiKey = "sk-abc123def456ghi789jkl012mno345pqr678stu"

attack_scenario.actor: Any attacker who can access the source code (e.g., via a public repository, insider access, or decompilation of a compiled binary).
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker obtains the source code (e.g., clones a public repo or extracts from a binary). 2. Attacker reads line 31 of main.go and extracts the string "sk-abc123def456ghi789jkl012mno345pqr678stu". 3. Attacker uses this key to authenticate to the corresponding API service (e.g., OpenAI, Stripe, etc.) and performs unauthorized actions.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker gains full access to the API service associated with the key, potentially incurring costs, accessing sensitive data, or performing actions on behalf of the application.
attack_scenario.explanation: The finding shows a hardcoded API key in source code. The key pattern 'sk-...' is typical for secret keys (e.g., OpenAI). Hardcoded secrets are a well-known vulnerability. The attacker only needs access to the code, which is common in open-source or leaked repositories. The impact is direct and severe.

findings.title: SBOM generated: 1 components detected
findings.description: Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) generated in CycloneDX format. 1 components inventoried for vulnerability tracking.

findings.title: Not a git repository — supply chain checks limited
findings.description: Supply chain security checks (SBOM provenance, SLSA) require a git repository. Without git history, we cannot verify commit signatures or build provenance.

findings.title: SBOM generation available via syft
findings.description: Run 'syft scan <target> -o cyclonedx-json' to generate a Software Bill of Materials. SBOMs are required for supply chain transparency (US Executive Order 14028).

findings.title: OpenSSF Scorecard not installed
findings.description: Install OpenSSF Scorecard (https://github.com/ossf/scorecard) to get automated security health checks for your dependencies and build pipeline.

findings.title: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) in proxy handler
findings.description: The handleProxy function takes a user-supplied URL via the 'url' query parameter and passes it directly to http.Get without any validation or allowlist. An attacker can make the server send requests to internal services (e.g., 169.254.169.254 for cloud metadata, localhost, internal APIs) or external systems, leading to information disclosure, port scanning, or further attacks.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement a strict allowlist of permitted hostnames or URL patterns. Validate the URL against the allowlist before making the request. Consider using a URL parser to extract and validate the host component.

findings.title: OS Command Injection in handleExec handler
findings.description: The handleExec function takes a 'cmd' query parameter from the user and passes it directly to exec.Command with 'bash -c', allowing arbitrary shell command execution. No authentication, input validation, or sanitization is performed.
findings.fix_suggestion: Avoid using shell interpreters with user input. If command execution is required, use a whitelist of allowed commands and arguments, or use exec.Command with separate arguments instead of 'bash -c'. Consider removing this endpoint entirely if not needed.

findings.title: Path Traversal in handleFiles
findings.description: User input from the 'filename' query parameter is concatenated directly into a file path without validation, allowing an attacker to read arbitrary files on the server via path traversal sequences (e.g., '../').
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate the filename to ensure it does not contain path traversal characters (e.g., '../' or '..\'). Use filepath.Base() to strip directory components, or maintain an allowlist of permitted filenames.

findings.title: Reflected Cross-Site Scripting in handleSearch
findings.description: User input from the 'query' query parameter is directly concatenated into an HTML string without escaping, then written to the response. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript by crafting a malicious query parameter, leading to reflected XSS.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use html/template to auto-escape output, or manually escape the query with html.EscapeString before embedding it in the HTML.

findings.title: Missing authentication on admin user list endpoint
findings.description: handleAdminUsers returns all user data without any authentication check. Anyone can access this endpoint and enumerate all users.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add auth middleware. Example: wrap handler with RequireAuth() or check session token at function start.
